title: The Body After Decades of Fawning
researcherTopic: polyvagal theory, neuroception, the social engagement system
metaDescription: Decades of fawning leave the body in a state of chronic activation. Learn how polyvagal theory explains this and how to begin the journey of embodiment.
imageDescription: A pair of gentle hands cradle a luminous, golden thread of light, weaving it through a delicate, abstract representation of the human nervous system. The background is soft and warm, suggesting a process of gentle repair and integration. The overall feeling is one of hope and profound healing.
htmlContent:
Stephen Porges spent decades mapping what your nervous system already knows ~ that safety is not a thought, but a sensation. It is a biological imperative, a non-negotiable prerequisite for every other function we associate with a life well-lived, from digestion to creativity to connection. For those who have endured years, or even decades, of navigating the world through a lens of complex trauma, the body’s innate capacity for this felt sense of safety can become a foreign country, a place glimpsed on maps but seemingly impossible to reach. It is the art of becoming a human tuning fork, vibrating at the precise frequency of another’s desire to ensure one’s own survival, and it leaves the body in a state of perpetual, low-grade activation that can persist long after the original danger has passed. This is the silent hum beneath the surface of daily life, the invisible architecture of a nervous system shaped by the relentless need to please, to pacify, to perform for safety.

The Architecture of Appeasement

The architecture of appeasement is not built in a day; it is constructed over a lifetime of small, repeated moments where expressing one’s own needs felt dangerous and attuning to another’s felt like the only path to survival. We learn to silence the protest in our own bodies, to swallow the "no" that rises in the throat, to arrange our own features into a mask of pleasant agreement, all in service of maintaining a fragile peace. In my years of working in this territory, I’ve sat with hundreds of people who describe this experience not as a conscious choice, but as a reflex as automatic as breathing. The fawn response, one of Pete Walker's four primary trauma responses, becomes less a strategy and more an identity, a way of being in the world that feels inseparable from who we are. We become so skilled at reading the micro-expressions of others, so adept at anticipating their needs, that we lose the ability to read our own internal terrain. The body becomes a ghost limb, a part of us we know is there but can no longer feel with any clarity.

What we find here is the core dilemma for the chronic fawner: the intellectual understanding that one is safe now does little to soothe a body that is still sensing threat everywhere. The body, in its genuine and ancient wisdom, does not listen to our thoughts; it listens to the symphony of sensations rising and falling within it. It remembers the subtle shift in a parent’s tone, the flicker of anger in a partner’s eyes, the atmospheric pressure of a room filled with unspoken tension. These are the data points that have shaped its operational map. To begin to unwind this conditioning is to begin the slow, painstaking work of teaching the body a new language, a language of safety that is not dependent on the approval of others. It is the work of learning to listen to the body’s own signals, not as problems to be managed, but as missives from a lost part of ourselves. What is the sensation of a genuine "yes" or a clear "no"? These are not questions we can answer with the mind. They must be felt, experienced, and embodied. It is a process from the head back down into the living, breathing reality of the organism, a return to the truth that was present before the performance began. We can find more on this in articles like The Tyranny of the 'Should'.

The High Cost of Hyper-Attunement

Neuroception: The Body’s Silent Sentinel

The path forward, then, is not to try to convince the mind that it is safe, but to provide the body with new experiences of safety. What we find here is the work of somatic therapies, of practices that invite us to turn our attention inward and to become curious about the terrain of our own sensations. It is about learning to track the subtle shifts in our own nervous system, to notice the moments of expansion and contraction, of activation and settling. It is in these small, quiet moments that we begin to build a new relationship with our own bodies. We learn that we can be with our own discomfort without being overwhelmed by it. This is the process of building what Porges calls a "vagal brake," the ability to consciously regulate our own nervous system and to bring ourselves back to a state of social engagement, of connection and calm. This is not about control, but about co-regulation with ourselves. It is a slow, tender process of re-parenting our own nervous system, of offering it the safety and attunement that it may never have received. This is a central theme in understanding The Geography of Grief.

The Slow Thaw of Embodiment

After decades of living from the neck up, the work back into the body can feel like a slow and sometimes painful thaw. As we begin to bring our attention inward, we may start to feel the full weight of the grief, the anger, and the fear that has been stored in our tissues for so long. That becomes not a sign that we are doing something wrong; it is a sign that the body is finally beginning to feel safe enough to speak its truth. To see them not as enemies to be vanquished, but as exiled parts of ourselves that are finally coming home. It is a process of reclamation, of gathering up the fragmented pieces of our own experience and holding them with a gentle and unwavering presence.

Which means the real work of healing happens, in the quiet, unglamorous moments of sitting with our own discomfort. It is not about achieving a state of perpetual bliss or of banishing all pain. It is about expanding our capacity to be with the full spectrum of our own human experience. It is about learning that we are vast enough to hold it all. As we continue on this path, we may begin to notice small shifts in our daily lives. These are not small things; they are seismic shifts in a nervous system that is learning a new way of being. They are the green shoots of a life that is no longer organized around the avoidance of threat, but around the pursuit of connection, of meaning, and of joy. It turns out to be the slow, steady work of embodiment, of coming home to ourselves, one breath at a time. It is a process that cannot be rushed, and it is a road that is unique to each of us.

Beyond the Performance of Wellness

In a culture that is obsessed with quick fixes and easy answers, the slow, deep work of healing complex trauma can feel counter-cultural. The wellness industry, in particular, often promotes a model of healing that is based on the idea of self-improvement, of becoming a better, shinier version of ourselves. But for those of us who have lived with the legacy of complex trauma, this approach can feel not only unhelpful, but also deeply invalidating. It can reinforce the very same message that we received in childhood: that there is something fundamentally wrong with us and that we need to be fixed.

Here is the uncomfortable truth: understanding this changes nothing. Only practice changes something. That is the work. Nobody said it would be comfortable.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do I still feel anxious even when I know I'm safe?

What emerges is the core paradox of a nervous system shaped by trauma. Your thinking mind may recognize safety, but your body's threat detection system, or neuroception, operates on a much older, more primal level. It's responding to decades of data that taught it to be on high alert, and it takes consistent, new experiences of felt safety - not just intellectual understanding ~ to recalibrate this system.

Is it possible to ever stop fawning completely?

The goal is not necessarily to eliminate the fawn response entirely, as it can be a useful social skill in small doses. The work is to move it from a compulsive, automatic reaction to a conscious choice. It's about expanding your capacity to access other responses - like healthy anger or setting a boundary - so that fawning is no longer your only option for navigating relationships.

Seriously.

How do I start listening to my body when it just feels numb?

Numbness is not the absence of feeling; it's a protective mechanism, a sign of overwhelming activation. Start small, with "islands of sensation." Can you feel the warmth of a cup in your hands for three seconds? These tiny moments of contact with the present moment are the entry points back into the body, slowly building its capacity to feel again without becoming overwhelmed.

What if setting boundaries makes me feel guilty or terrified?

That becomes a completely normal and expected part of the process. For a nervous system conditioned to appease, a boundary can feel like a declaration of war. The key is to start with very small, low-stakes boundaries and to have a plan for how you will self-regulate and care for yourself after you set one, reminding your system that you have survived the experience.

The body that has learned to fawn is a body that has learned to equate survival with self-abandonment. It is a body that has become a stranger to its own needs, its own desires, its own truth. The process home is not a path of fixing or of changing, but of remembering. It is the work of a lifetime, and it is the most important work we will ever do. What would it be to trust the wisdom of your own organism, even when it contradicts everything you have been taught to believe?
